entry.title: ISYE6650
entry.content:
Probabilistic Models

Hexo图片插入太不方便了！（这篇review当时有点赶，所以都插的图片） Anyway, I have my own blog now lol.

Lecture 4 Discrete-time Markov Chains Review

1 Defining Markov Chain

Definition of Markov Chain

Conception: 1 Process 2 State 3 State space 4 Trajectory

Definition of Transition Probabilities of MC

Columns don’t add to 1 generally. But when it does, it satisfies the following requirements:

Examples

1 Forecasting the Weather

Depends on the last day whether or not it is raining

Example 4.1 page 184

2 A Communication System

Example 4.2 page 184

3 How to create a new Markov chain where one of the states is absorbing:

Example 4.3 page 184

Exercise 10\11

4 Transforming a Process into a Markov Chain

Example 4.4 page 185

5 A Random Walk Model

$P_{i,i+1}=p=1-P_{i,i-1}, i=0,\pm1,…$

6 A Gambling Model

$P_{i,i+1}=p=1-P_{i,i-1}, i=1,2,…,N-1$

$P_{00}=P_{NN}=1$

Note that the preceding is a finite state random walk with absorbing barriers(State 0 and N)

2 n-step Transition Probabilities

Definition 3

Chapman-Kolmogorov Equations

n-step Transition Matrix

Unconditional Probabilities

3 Classification of States

Basic conception

Communication is an equivalence relation

Class of States

Irreducible Markov Chain

Recurrent and Transient Markov Chain

Properties of Recurrent and Transient MC

For example:

If Markov Chain has three classes, e.g. , $\left{0,1\right}$ $\left{2,3\right}$ $\left{4\right}$, then each class is either recurrent or transient.

If Markov Chain is irreducible, then this chain is either recurrent or transient.

Mark: Not all states in a finite Markov chain can be transient.

Example for determining classes of states

Remarks: All states communicate, so this chain is irreducible.

4 Stationary Distribution of MC

Limiting Probabilities

We can see that no matter which state initially is, $\pi_0$ and $\pi_1$ for each initial states are the same.

Examples

Long-run proportions are not always limiting probabilities

Periodic vs Aperiodic

Key results so far

Finite

Remarks: Point 2 shows that $\pi_j$ doesn’t depend on initial states $i$

Infinite

Positive recurrent vs Null recurrent

==***====For example==:

A little resemblance with random walk but the state has no negative figure.

And also a little resemblance with gambling model but this one has infinite states.

Absorbing states

The Gambler’s Ruin Problem

Description:

Thinking:

Solution:

For example

5 Mean time spent in transient states

For example

Lecture 5 The Exponential Distribution and the Poisson Process

1 The Exponential Distribution

Definition

CDF\PDF\E(X)\Var(X)

Properties

Memoryless

Example:

Further properties

The failure rate function

2 The Poisson Process

Counting Process

Two concepts:

Independent increments:

If the numbers of events that occur in disjoint time intervals are independent.

Stationary increments:

If the distribution of the number of events that occur in any interval of time depends only on the length of the time interval. In other words, the process has stationary increments if the number of events in the interval $(s,s+t)$ has the same distribution for all $s$

Definition of the Poisson Process

1 one of the important types of counting process $N(t)$

2 极小量 $o(h)$:

3 Definition of the Poisson Process:

5.3.3 Interarrival and Waiting Time Distributions

***Please note that N(t+s)-N(t) is Poisson distribution; and the following $T_n$ are i.i.d exponential random variables.

维基百科：

Gamma function

Waiting Time

5.3.4 Further Properties of Poisson Process

(Optional) It is about the proof.

Conditional distribution of arrival time

Order statistics

Waiting time order statistics

It means every Si has the i.i.d uniform distribution like f(t) = 1/t (Si is the order statistics with uniform distribution)

Sampling a Poisson Process

上面没懂 :(

PPT后面Example 5.20 记得在上面弄懂后去看

非重点

5.4.1 Nonhomogeneous Poisson Process

5.4.2 Compound Poisson Process

Lecture 6 Queueing Theory

记住lambda_a 的定义对后面的理解有帮助

8.2.2 Steady-State Probabilities

PASTA Principle

​ $a_n=\lambda P_n/\lambda = P_n$

8.3 Exponential Model

8.3.1 A Single-Server Exponential Queueing System

Back to our discussion:

M/M/1 Queueing System with Balking

M/M/c Queue

entry.title: Hello World
entry.content:
Welcome to Hexo ! This is your very first post. Check documentation for more info. If you get any problems when using Hexo, you can find the answer in troubleshooting or you can ask me on GitHub .

Quick Start

Create a new post

More info: Writing

Run server

More info: Server

Generate static files

More info: Generating

Deploy to remote sites

More info: Deployment
